summary: Gruber highlights the release of BBEdit 7.1, which includes 102 fixes and three significant new features: built-in Webkit HTML previewing, SFTP support, and Rendezvous support. He notes that all three new features only work on Mac OS X, even though BBEdit 7.1 still supports Mac OS 9. He frames this as a natural consequence of progress rather than abandonment of legacy users, since the features depend on OS X-only system services. He closes with a humorous note about his own easter egg in BBEdit's About box.
key_points: BBEdit 7.1's three major new features—Webkit preview, SFTP, and Rendezvous—are all Mac OS X-only despite the app still supporting OS 9
The OS X exclusivity of new features reflects the reality of platform capabilities, not developer laziness
Backwards compatibility should not come at the expense of progress
money_quotes: Backwards compatibility is noble, but not at the expense of progress.
That's not a sign of disdain or laziness on the part of Bare Bones's programmers — it's just reality.
Also, more importantly, the "Gruber" easter egg in the About BBEdit box still works.
key_insight: Maintaining backward compatibility is admirable, but the best software moves forward by embracing the capabilities of modern platforms rather than being held back by legacy ones.
